summary: Gruber builds on Matt Birchler's analysis of the poor state of 'enthusiast apps' on Android to argue that the quality gap between iOS and Android apps is growing, not shrinking. He contends that Android apps are aesthetically unpolished and poorly designed even from a functional perspective, using the RSS reader Read You as a representative example. When challenged by Android users who demand 'measurable criteria' for evaluating app quality, Gruber argues that software can and should be art, and that demanding quantifiable metrics for design quality is like demanding measurable criteria for evaluating a movie or painting. He theorizes that users and developers have self-sorted over the past decade: those who value the artistic dimension of software have gravitated to iOS, while those who don't have ended up on Android. He closes by invoking Steve Jobs's famous declaration that Apple exists at the intersection of technology and the liberal arts.
key_points: The best app in any category on Android would rank 3rd to 5th best on iOS, and this gap is widening over time
Demanding 'measurable criteria' for software design quality misses the point — software can be art, and art resists quantification
People who prioritize design can understand those who prioritize other factors, but the reverse is rarely true — design-skeptics literally don't perceive what they're missing
Users and developers have self-sorted by platform over the past decade, creating two ecosystems with fundamentally different cultural values
The iOS-Android divide is not a Coke-vs-Pepsi preference but a deep cultural polarization around whether software should aspire to be art
money_quotes: Android enthusiasts don't want to hear it, but from a design perspective, the apps on Android suck.
That's like asking for 'measurable criteria' for evaluating a movie or novel or song or painting.
Either you know that software can be art, and often should be, or you think what I'm talking about here is akin to astrology.
People who prioritize design, UI, and UX in the software they prefer can empathize with and understand the choices made by people who prioritize other factors. But it doesn't work the other way.
Most people who prioritize other things can't fathom why anyone cares deeply about design/UI/UX because they don't perceive it.
Those who see and value the artistic value in software and interface design have overwhelmingly wound up on iOS; those who don't have wound up on Android.
On mobile, the cultural differences are as polarized and clearly delineated as the politics of our national affairs.
The test of a work of art is, in the end, our affection for it, not our ability to explain why it is good.
key_insight: The growing quality gap between iOS and Android apps reflects a deep cultural self-sorting: people who see software as art have gravitated to iOS, while those who don't perceive design have landed on Android, and this divide is only widening.
